fingerprint: O1's period (25.82h) encodes the moon's sidereal orbital period (27.32 days) via the Doodson decomposition — an orbital quantity that has no dome derivation
globe_value: O1 angular speed σ(O1) = τ − s, where τ = 14.4921°/h (mean lunar time = 360°/24.84h) and s = 0.5490°/h (moon's sidereal orbital rate = 360°/(27.3217 × 24h)). T(O1) = 360°/13.9431°/h = 25.8193h. The subtraction of 's' is the moon's eastward orbital motion through the stellar background — a consequence of the moon orbiting Earth at 384,400 km with a 27.32-day period governed by Kepler's third law (T² = 4π²a³/GM). On the globe, O1 is geometrically slower than the lunar day (24.84h) because the moon advances ~13.2° eastward per day along its orbit, requiring an extra 0.98h for the tidal forcing to 'catch up.'
dome_value: 25.82h appears ONLY as a static text entry on wins.html: 'O1 principal lunar diurnal tidal constituent = 25.8200 hours (NOAA standard value). Result: Exact match. Zero error.' No formula, no computation, no monitoring domain. monitor.py tracks M2 (=LUNAR_H/2), K1 (=SIDEREAL), and S2 (=SOLAR_H/2) but NOT O1. inject_ai_layer.py, predictions.js, scoring.js, formulas.json, parameters.json, and model.html contain zero O1 references.
match_precision: 0.0% — identity (dome cites 25.82h, observed is 25.82h, both are the NOAA standard value)
why_it_matters: O1 requires knowing the moon's sidereal orbital rate (s = 0.5490°/h = one orbit per 27.32 days). On the globe, this orbital period arises from Kepler's third law at a distance of 384,400 km. On the dome, the moon at 2,534 km altitude has no orbital mechanics — OPEN-007 explicitly concedes 'moon orbital mechanics require a different framework.' The dome cannot derive WHY the moon takes 27.32 days to complete its sidereal cycle, and therefore cannot derive O1's period from dome parameters. The dome's monitor.py can handle M2 (=LUNAR_H/2), K1 (=SIDEREAL), and S2 (=SOLAR_H/2) because these are trivial halving operations on adopted constants. But O1 requires the Doodson decomposition: σ(O1) = τ − s, which subtracts the moon's orbital angular speed from the lunar transit rate. This subtraction has no physical meaning on a flat disc where the moon merely circuits overhead — there is no 'eastward orbital motion through the stellar background' without a spherical celestial geometry. This is likely why O1 is absent from monitor.py despite being claimed as a WIN: the dome's code authors could not construct even a nominal formula to justify the period.

additional_fingerprints.K1_O1_bracket: K1 (23.93h) and O1 (25.82h) bracket the solar day (24.00h) and lunar day (24.84h) respectively, from opposite sides. K1 < 24.00h because Earth's annual solar orbit adds one sidereal rotation per year. O1 > 24.84h because the moon's monthly orbit subtracts one lunar transit per month. Both offsets are orbital in origin: K1 encodes the Earth-Sun orbital relationship, O1 encodes the Earth-Moon orbital relationship. On the dome, neither the sun's nor the moon's circuit has an orbital counterpart — the firmament, sun, and moon are independent structures with no geometric reason for either offset. The dome adopts SIDEREAL = 23.9345h and LUNAR_H = 24.84h as bare constants, never deriving why they differ from 24.00h and ~24.00h respectively.
additional_fingerprints.O1_frequency_relation: In the Doodson system: f(O1) = f(M2)/2 − f(month), where f(month) = 1/(27.3217 × 24h). This means O1's frequency is exactly the M2 half-frequency minus the monthly lunar orbital frequency. The dome computes M2 = LUNAR_H/2 but never performs this further step. The 'missing link' is f(month) — the moon's orbital frequency — which the dome cannot justify from a disc geometry.
additional_fingerprints.18.61_year_nodal_modulation: O1's amplitude varies on an 18.61-year cycle (node factor 0.80–1.19) due to the regression of the moon's orbital nodes. This is the same period claimed as WIN-020. The dome has no mechanism for nodal regression (WIN-020 was found to have zero epicyclic code, with moon_dec formula using fixed 28.6° amplitude — see globe-fingerprints/WIN-020.json). So even if the dome could somehow justify O1's period, it cannot predict O1's amplitude modulation.

code_search.found_in: docs/wins.html ONLY: WIN-050 listed as 'O1 principal lunar diurnal tidal constituent = 25.8200 hours (NOAA standard value). Result: Exact match. Zero error.' Status: Confirmed (2026-03-23). Claim: 'In the ECM framework, this period emerges directly from the moon's circuit mechanics above the flat plane, with no gravitational perturbation theory required.'
code_search.notes: O1 is absent from monitor.py (which tracks M2, K1, S2 only), absent from inject_ai_layer.py confirmed_wins, absent from predictions.js, absent from formulas.json (133 formulas, none tidal), absent from parameters.json, absent from model.html. The wins.html entry claims O1 'emerges directly from the moon's circuit mechanics' but no formula, derivation, or computation exists anywhere in the repository to support this claim. This is the most purely bibliographic WIN in the tidal cluster — even M2 gets a trivial LUNAR_H/2 computation in monitor.py, but O1 gets nothing. The dome code authors likely realized they could not construct O1 = f(dome_params) because it requires the moon's sidereal orbital rate, which has no dome derivation.

kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies O1 as the principal lunar diurnal constituent with the standard period of 25.82h. The wins.html entry correctly describes it as a 'lunar diurnal' constituent and correctly states the NOAA standard value. Including O1 alongside M2/K1/S2 shows awareness of the four major tidal harmonics. The claim that the period 'emerges from moon's circuit mechanics' contains a grain of truth: O1 does arise from the relationship between the moon's motion and Earth's rotation. But the dome's 'circuit mechanics' (moon at 2,534 km orbiting the disc) cannot reproduce the specific angular speed subtraction (τ − s) that defines O1 in the Doodson system.
suggested_text: O1's period of 25.82 hours encodes the moon's sidereal orbital period (27.32 days) via the Doodson tidal decomposition: σ(O1) = τ − s, where τ is the lunar transit rate and s is the moon's orbital angular speed (0.549°/h). On the globe, this orbital period arises from Kepler's third law at 384,400 km. The dome's monitor.py can handle M2 (= LUNAR_H/2), K1 (= SIDEREAL), and S2 (= SOLAR_H/2) — trivial halving operations on adopted constants — but O1 requires subtracting the moon's orbital angular speed from the transit rate, a computation that has no physical meaning on a flat disc. This is likely why O1 is absent from monitor.py despite being listed as a confirmed WIN: the dome cannot construct even a nominal derivation.
